macro_segments.title: Recap of the Yochanan-Reish Lakish Debate on Kinyan and the Conceptual Status of Money Payment

micro_segments.title: Summary of the previous debate: Is meshicha or kesef the operative kinyan?
Two interpretations of Rabbi Yochanan's position: Does kesef become ineffective or does it remain binding with a right to retract?
The role of Rabbi Shimon's position in clarifying that money payment creates bindingness

macro_segments.title: The Question of Koneh K'neged Kullo versus K'negdo in Monetary Transactions

micro_segments.title: Application to karka: partial payment counts as full purchase with a loan for the remainder
Application to metaltelin: the debate between Rav and Rabbi Yochanan on whether partial payment creates rights to the whole or only to the paid portion
The significance of the question: When does the buyer get a mi shepara if retracting on the unpaid portion?

macro_segments.title: The Tannaitic Debate on Mashkon and Shemittah: Evidence for Koneh K'neged Kullo or K'negdo

micro_segments.title: The case of a loan with collateral worth less than the loan, and whether shemittah annuls the entire loan or only the unpaid portion
Interpretation of Rabbi Shimon ben Gamliel: koneh k'neged kullo—holding the mashkon counts as holding the entire loan
Interpretation of Rabbi Yehuda HaNasi: koneh k'negdo—shemittah only applies to the unpaid portion
The Gemara's resolution: the mashkon represents only a reminder, not full possession, so the debate concerns whether it constitutes possession of the loan's value

macro_segments.title: The Rav Kahana Case: Partial Payment, Verbal Commitment, and the Absence of Mi Shepara

micro_segments.title: The scenario: Rav Kahana received partial payment for flax and wanted to back out when prices rose
Rav's ruling: koneh k'negdo (only the paid portion is binding) and devarim ein bahem mishum mechusrei amanah (verbal commitment on unpaid portion is not a violation of trust)

macro_segments.title: The Debate on Devarim and Violation of Trust: Rav versus Rabbi Yochanan

micro_segments.title: Itmar: The position of Rav (devarim ein bahem mishum mechusrei amanah) versus Rabbi Yochanan (devarim yesh bahem mishum mechusrei amanah)
Rabbi Yose bar Yehuda's teaching on 'Hin Tzedek': your yes must be yes and your no must be no—establishing obligation to keep commitments
Abaye's response: 'Not saying one thing and another in your heart' means you cannot be duplicitous when making the promise, but changing your mind later is permissible

macro_segments.title: The Rabbi Yochanan ben Matzya Story: The Limits of Reliance and Retraction Before Performance Begins

micro_segments.title: The case: hiring workers for a set wage plus meals, and the father's instruction to clarify terms before work begins
The Gemara's analysis: the workers had not fully relied on the commitment because they understood the father's agreement was pending
Principle: violation of trust (mechusrei amanah) depends on whether the other side actually relied on the commitment

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's Teaching on Gifts and the Limits of Mechusrei Amanah

micro_segments.title: The case: promising to give a gift (matanah mu'etet) and retracting—Rabbi Yochanan permits retraction only when reliance is not significant
Rav Papa's reading: Rabbi Yochanan agrees that when the other side truly relies (samcha da'ataiyu), there is mechusrei amanah even without opportunity loss

macro_segments.title: The Levi and Maaser Case: Moral Obligation Without Legal Bindingness

micro_segments.title: The case: A Yisrael promises a Levi a measure of maaser from his threshing floor, and the Levi uses it to separate trumat maaser on his own produce
The issue: The Levi can only reliably separate maaser if confident the promise will be kept—this supports Rabbi Yochanan's position that verbal commitments bind morally when relied upon
The resolution: If retraction were permitted, the Levi might separate maaser on produce he never receives, eating terumah (tevel), proving the promise must hold at least until performance

macro_segments.title: The Sesame Seeds Story: Retraction, Shomer Status, and Mi Shepara

micro_segments.title: The case: Money is given for sesame seeds, prices rise, the seller wants to back out and offers to return the money, but the buyer refuses to take it back and the money is stolen
Rava's ruling: Since the buyer refused to accept the money back, the seller is not even a shomer chinam on it—neither liable for loss nor capable of using it
The Rabbis' pushback: The sale is not reversed until the other side accepts mi shepara—if not accepted, the sale remains binding and the seller is liable for the money
Rav Tuvyumi's account: A seller's personal testimony that he never would retract, and clarification that the story involved deposit (pikadon), not a sale retraction

macro_segments.title: The Liability Question: Shomer Status After Money Payment and Before Meshicha

micro_segments.title: The debate among rishonim: Once money is paid, is the recipient a shomer, or does the money become the buyer's property in the form of a mortgage/loan?
The prevailing view: Money payment creates a binding sale that can be reversed, making the seller liable for the money like a ba'al (owner) rather than a shomer

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Rule: Only the Recipient of Money Can Retract

micro_segments.title: The rule: 'Kol she-hakessef be-yado yado al ha-elyona'—whoever holds the money has the right to retract
The case: Money in seller's hands, fruit in buyer's hands—the seller cannot retract because the buyer already has the object

Gemara's reading: 'In the hands' does not mean literal possession but rather the buyer doesn't have meshicha; however, if the buyer is in the property (renting from seller), the buyer can protect against loss despite not having meshicha

macro_segments.title: The Wine Seizure Case: Good Cause for Retraction and Absence of Mi Shepara

micro_segments.title: The case: A buyer gives money for wine, hears the government will seize it, and wants to retract without taking possession
Rav Chisda's ruling: Both seller and buyer can retract before meshicha; retracting due to government seizure (serious loss) does not incur mi shepara
The principle: A mi shepara is imposed for arbitrary retraction in normal market conditions, but not when protecting against catastrophic loss or serious reliance is minimal

topical_tags.term: koneh k'negdo (purchasing only the portion paid)
mi shepara (divine punishment for breaking word)
